text: mpactr provides filters to identify and remove mispicked ions, which are isotopic patterns that are incorrectly split during preprocessing.
source_excerpt: mispicked ions: isotopic patterns that are incorrectly split during preprocessing.

text: The filter_mispicked_ions function checks for similar ions based on retention time and mass using the parameters ringwin, isowin, trwin and max_iso_shift.
source_excerpt: This function takes an `mpactr object` as input, and checks for similar ions with the arguments `ringwin`, `isowin`, `trwin` and `max_iso_shift`.

text: mpactr is built on an R6 class-system, meaning it operates on reference semantics in which data is updated in-place.
source_excerpt: mpactr is built on an R6 class-system, meaning it operates on reference semantics in which data is updated *in-place*.

text: Reference semantics in R6 classes differ from shallow copy, where only data pointers are copied, and from deep copy, where the entire data object is copied in memory.
source_excerpt: Compared to a shallow copy, where only data pointers are copied, or a deep copy, where the entire data object is copied in memory, any changes to the original data object, regardless if they are assigned to a new object, result in changes to the original data object.

text: The default setting for the copy_object parameter in mpactr filters is FALSE, which operates on reference semantics.
source_excerpt: We can run the `filter_mispicked_ions` filter, with default setting `copy_object = FALSE` (operates on reference semantics).

text: Using the default copy_object = FALSE makes for an extremely fast and memory-efficient way to chain mpactr filters together.
source_excerpt: We recommend using the default `copy_object = FALSE` as this makes for an extremely fast and memory-efficient way to chain mpactr filters together

text: mpactr uses R6 environments to enable reference semantics and fast filter execution.
source_excerpt: R6 Accomplishes this by taking advantaging of the environment system in R.

text: Functions in R rely on call-by-value semantics where parameterized values are treated as local variables.
source_excerpt: In R, functions rely on call-by-value semantics. Call-by-value is described as functions treating parameterized values (values specified when calling the function) as local variables when in the function.

text: R6 classes allow sending variables by reference to functions using environment semantics.
source_excerpt: you can send an environment to a function, and it will not copy the environment. This allows you to send variables by reference to functions. R6 classes rely on this

text: Memory usage is improved when using R6 classes compared to traditional copy-by-value workflows.
source_excerpt: Memory usage really shines when you use R6 classes vs. a traditional workflow, such as copy by
